reviews.review_description:
Zombie Survival Protocol is a wave-based tunnel crawler where each passage brings new tension and tougher undead. You clear tunnels by any means necessary, loot hidden items from wooden crates (pro tip: use the crossbar to save ammo), and move forward into the next claustrophobic challenge.

The starting laser sight makes headshots satisfying and effective, especially in those pitch-black corridors. There’s also a clever layer of strategy — your flashlight drains over time, so scavenging for batteries is crucial unless you want a nasty surprise (and an untimely death).

A well-designed, atmospheric survival game that keeps the pressure on and rewards precision and resource management.

VR + Zombies + Weapons.... Need I say More? We played this fun little Zombie game where you move one section at a time shooting zombies and getting items from crates.  Manage your ammo and other inventory for if you run out it is down to the weak fists or crowbar.  Every few sections you'll have a little mini boss zombie where you have to take it and all the other zombies out before you can move on. Enjoy this Never Ending zombie shooter as you try to beat my, I mean everyone's High Score!

Important thing to know: zombies can only be killed when their eyes turn red. You must use a flashlight or glow stick first, otherwise your bullets do nothing. Once you learn this, the combat becomes very tactical.
At first I thought the game was brutal, until I realized only red-eyed zombies can be killed. Using light to expose them is essential, and missing that mechanic means you won’t survive long.

This game is all about tension. Limited ammo, dark corridors, and enemies that don’t always need to be fought make every decision feel important and stressful.
Procedurally generated tunnels keep the experience fresh. You never know what’s around the next corner, and the atmosphere stays intense even after many runs.
The "bosses" are funny when they're angry.

The thing I didn’t realize at first is that zombies only die when their eyes turn red — so you have to hit them with your flashlight or glow stick first. Makes you actually pay attention instead of just shooting everything. The tunnels feel endless and scary, ammo is limited, and the journal pages slowly tell a story, which is nice. It’s stressful, but in a good way — like every choice matters, and you actually feel like you survived something. And you can actually switch language by pressing left menu button.
The story is surprisingly good. Finding journal pages in the tunnels slowly reveals what happened, and it adds emotional weight to the constant danger and loneliness.
